items.title: Impact of Age and Gender on Breast Cancer Risk
items.content: Female gender and increasing age are significant non-genetic risk factors for breast cancer. The majority of cases occur in women over 55, with a median diagnosis age of 63.
keyPoints: 70% of breast cancer is diagnosed in women aged 55 years and older.
Median age at diagnosis is 63.
Incidence highest among non-Hispanic whites; mortality highest in non-Hispanic blacks.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Breast Cancer | NONGENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.title: Role of Estrogen Exposure and Other Risk Factors in Breast Cancer
items.content: Prolonged exposure to estrogen, through factors like early menarche, late menopause, and hormone replacement therapy, increases breast cancer risk. Postmenopausal obesity also contributes due to increased estrogen levels.
keyPoints: Early menarche, late menopause, and late first pregnancy are risk factors.
Prolonged use of hormone replacement therapy (not estrogen alone) is associated with higher risk.
Postmenopausal obesity increases risk; premenopausal does not.
Alcohol consumption is a confirmed risk factor.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Breast Cancer | NONGENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.title: Impact of Family History and Hereditary Genes on Breast Cancer Risk
items.content: Family history significantly influences breast cancer risk. A diagnosis in a first-degree relative doubles the risk, while personal history of invasive breast cancer increases the likelihood of developing new breast cancers. Genetic mutations, particularly BRCA1 and BRCA2, account for 5-10% of cases and confer a high lifetime risk (50-80%) of invasive breast cancer by age 80.
keyPoints: 20% of women with breast cancer have a family history.
First-degree relative diagnosis doubles breast cancer risk.
BRCA1/2 mutations impart 50–80% risk of invasive breast cancer by age 80.
5-10% of breast cancers are due to hereditary susceptibility genes.
BRCA1 (17q21) and BRCA2 (13q12) are common mutation sites.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.title: Guidelines for Genetic Testing in Breast Cancer Susceptibility
items.content: Genetic testing for breast cancer susceptibility is recommended based on specific criteria, including personal and family history. It's advised for individuals with early-onset breast cancer or those with a strong family history of related cancers. Testing should be preceded by appropriate counseling.
keyPoints: Testing not recommended for the general population.
Consider testing for high-penetrance genes in patients diagnosed ≤65 years.
Offer testing to older patients with Ashkenazi Jewish ancestry or specific cancer types.
Include family history of early-onset, male breast cancer, ovarian, pancreatic, or metastatic prostate cancer.
Use risk assessment tools like Tyrer-Cuzick score for higher-risk individuals.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.title: Prophylactic Mastectomy Significantly Reduces Breast Cancer Risk in High-Risk Individuals
items.content: Prophylactic mastectomy is a highly effective preventive measure for women with germline breast cancer susceptibility gene mutations, reducing the risk of developing breast cancer by about 90%. However, it should be discouraged in women with low or only modest risk due to limited impact on outcomes.
keyPoints: Reduces breast cancer risk by ~90% in mutation carriers
Breast cancer may still develop in residual tissue post-surgery
Not recommended for women with low/modest risk
items.reference: Breast Cancer · PREVENTION | PROPHYLACTIC SURGERY | CHEMOPREVENTION

items.title: Chemoprevention Strategies Targeting Estrogen Signaling Reduce Breast Cancer Risk
items.content: Tamoxifen and aromatase inhibitors are effective chemopreventive agents for high-risk women, targeting estrogen signaling pathways. Tamoxifen reduces invasive breast cancer risk in women aged ≥60 years or those with LCIS, while aromatase inhibitors reduce incidence by ~50% in postmenopausal women at moderate-high risk.
keyPoints: Tamoxifen reduces risk for women ≥60 years or with LCIS
Risk reduction threshold: 1.67% over 5 years
Aromatase inhibitors (exemestane, anastrozole) reduce incidence by ~50% in postmenopausal women at moderate-high risk
Raloxifene is an alternative for postmenopausal women with fewer uterine cancers
items.reference: Breast Cancer · PREVENTION | PROPHYLACTIC SURGERY | CHEMOPREVENTION

items.title: Impact of Lifestyle Modifications on Breast Cancer Risk
items.content: Certain lifestyle modifications can potentially reduce breast cancer risk. These include maintaining a normal body mass index, avoiding alcohol consumption, and minimizing the use of hormone replacement therapy. Regular exercise during adolescence may also be beneficial.
keyPoints: Maintaining a normal BMI is associated with reduced breast cancer risk.
Avoidance of alcohol can lower breast cancer risk.
Minimizing hormone replacement therapy usage reduces risk.
Regular adolescent exercise might reduce breast cancer incidence.
Low-fat diet in postmenopausal women showed a nonsignificant reduction in breast cancer incidence but appeared to reduce mortality from the disease.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION | SCREENING

items.title: Breast Cancer Screening Guidelines and Modalities
items.content: Breast cancer screening involves various modalities, with mammography being most studied. It reduces breast cancer mortality by 20–25% in women aged ≥50 years. Clinical breast exams (CBE) every 2 years have shown a significant reduction in late-stage diagnoses but only a nonsignificant 15% reduction in mortality. MRI is recommended for high-risk individuals, while the role of ultrasound and MCED tests remains less clear.
keyPoints: Screening mammography reduces breast cancer mortality by about 20–25% in women ≥50 years.
CBE every 2 years significantly reduced stage III or IV diagnoses but had a nonsignificant 15% reduction in mortality.
Most guidelines recommend mammography every 1–3 years for women aged 50–70 years, with some recommending it for those aged 40–50 as well.
MRI is suggested for women with ≥20% lifetime risk of breast cancer or very dense breasts on mammography.
MCED tests have not shown to replace conventional screening; mammographic screening should still be performed.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION | SCREENING

items.title: Importance of Imaging Both Breasts During Breast Cancer Diagnosis
items.content: When diagnosing breast cancer, it is crucial to image both breasts because up to 3% of women with newly diagnosed breast cancer have unsuspected contralateral disease.
keyPoints: Up to 3% of patients with newly diagnosed breast cancer may have contralateral (opposite side) disease.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.title: Staging and Management Decisions in Breast Cancer
items.content: Breast cancer staging is essential as it informs therapy decisions and provides insights into the natural history of the disease. The TNM system, incorporating molecular testing such as biomarkers and genomic tests like Oncotype 21 gene assay, guides treatment strategies.
keyPoints: Staging uses the TNM (tumor-node-metastasis) system, which includes molecular testing.
Oncotype 21 gene assay is part of molecular testing in staging.
Stages I and II are early-stage disease confined to breast and ipsilateral nodes.
Stage III represents locally advanced breast cancer.
Stage IV denotes de novo metastatic breast cancer (M1).
Common metastasis sites: soft tissues, lung, bone, liver, brain.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.title: Diverse Subtypes and Treatment Implications in Invasive Breast Cancer
items.content: Invasive breast cancer is classified into multiple molecular subtypes, each with distinct therapeutic implications. Luminal A and B tumors express ER and are responsive to antiestrogen strategies, but luminal B has reduced PR expression or increased proliferation (Ki67), leading to poorer outcomes. HER2-positive cancers benefit from targeted therapies, while TNBCs, which lack ER, PR, and HER2, have a worse prognosis and are treated with chemotherapy and immunotherapy.
keyPoints: Invasive breast cancer has at least four major molecular subtypes: Luminal A, Luminal B, HER2-positive, and TNBC.
Luminal B tumors show reduced PR expression or increased proliferation (Ki67) and have poorer outcomes than luminal A.
HER2 subtype benefits from HER2-targeted therapies.
TNBCs lack ER, PR, and HER2 expression, carry a poorer prognosis, and are managed with chemotherapy and immunotherapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.title: Pathologic Evaluation and Natural History of Lobular Breast Cancer
items.content: Lobular breast cancers, constituting 10-15% of invasive cases, are typically ER positive and HER2 negative. They lack E-cadherin staining or function, leading to a propensity for metastasis to serosal surfaces like the omentum, pleura, meninges, ovaries, and gastrointestinal tracts. This can result in unusual clinical presentations that may delay diagnosis.
keyPoints: Lobular breast cancers make up 10-15% of invasive cases and are usually ER positive and HER2 negative.
They lack E-cadherin staining or function.
Propensity for metastasis to serosal surfaces can lead to unusual clinical presentations.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.title: Genomic Profiling in Early-Stage ER-Positive Breast Cancer
items.content: Early-stage ER-positive breast cancers can be evaluated using genomic transcriptional profiling assays to determine if adjuvant chemotherapy is necessary alongside endocrine therapy. Five recommended assays by the American Society of Clinical Oncology include Oncotype Dx (21 genes), MammaPrint (70 genes), ProSigna (50 genes), EndoPredict (12 genes), and Breast Cancer Index (two genes plus a proliferation signature). These assays are not directly comparable, so using more than one for a single case is discouraged.
keyPoints: Genomic profiling assays help determine the necessity of adjuvant chemotherapy in early-stage ER-positive breast cancers.
Five recommended assays: Oncotype Dx (21 genes), MammaPrint (70 genes), ProSigna (50 genes), EndoPredict (12 genes), Breast Cancer Index (two genes plus a proliferation signature).
Assays are not directly comparable; using more than one for a single case is discouraged.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER
items.title: Management of Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS)
items.content: The management of DCIS involves excision to negative margins and confirmation of no invasion. Radiotherapy may be used post-excision, particularly if the DCIS is ER-positive, to reduce recurrence risk. Extensive DCIS might necessitate mastectomy, while small lesions can be managed with excision and surveillance.
keyPoints: Untreated DCIS has a 30% risk of developing invasive cancer in the same breast.
Excision aims for negative margins; axillary lymph node evaluation is not standard.
Radiotherapy may follow excision, especially if ER-positive.
Mastectomy is considered for extensive DCIS; small lesions can be managed with surveillance.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER
items.title: Management of Lobular Carcinoma In Situ (LCIS)
items.content: LCIS is often found incidentally and indicates a higher risk for invasive breast cancer, with 25-30% developing it in either breast. Management includes continued screening and possibly chemoprevention with tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors. Bilateral prophylactic mastectomy may be considered for those with additional risk factors.
keyPoints: LCIS is associated with a 25–30% risk of subsequent invasive cancer in either breast.
Management focuses on continued screening and chemoprevention options like tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors.
Bilateral prophylactic mastectomy is an option for those with additional risks.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER

items.title: Breast-Conservation Therapy vs. Mastectomy: Survival Equivalence
items.content: For early-stage breast cancer, both breast-conservation therapy (lumpectomy with radiotherapy) and modified radical mastectomy offer equivalent survival outcomes.
keyPoints: Six randomized trials confirm equivalent survival for breast-conservation therapy or modified radical mastectomy in early-stage breast cancer.
Contraindications to breast conservation include patient preference, poor cosmesis, multifocal disease, previous chest radiation, and ongoing pregnancy preventing timely radiotherapy.
BRCA gene mutations may influence the decision towards bilateral mastectomy due to high risk of subsequent disease.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.title: Axillary Management: Sentinel Node Localization and Dissection
items.content: Sentinel node localization is used for staging in patients with small tumors and clinically negative axillary nodes, while axillary dissection is reserved for those with palpable lymph nodes or significant sentinel node involvement.
keyPoints: Sentinel node localization identifies relevant nodes for removal and pathologic assessment to inform therapy decisions.
Axillary dissection involves level 1 and 2 lymph nodes, used when there are palpable lymph nodes or substantial sentinel node involvement.
Omission of sentinel node evaluation is supported in older women with small biologically favorable tumors undergoing adjuvant endocrine therapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.title: Postoperative Radiotherapy: Tailored Strategies and Omission Criteria
items.content: Postoperative radiotherapy strategies are tailored based on individual factors, with options including whole breast radiation or hypofractionated regimens. Omitting radiotherapy is safe for older women with low-risk ER-positive tumors receiving endocrine therapy.
keyPoints: Radiotherapy options include standard daily WBR for 4–6 weeks, hypofractionated schedules, and partial breast radiation.
Clinical trials show no benefit of partial breast radiation over whole breast radiation.
Omitting radiotherapy is safe for women older than 65 with low-risk ER-positive tumors on endocrine therapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.title: Indications for Neoadjuvant Therapy in Breast Cancer
items.content: Neoadjuvant therapy is considered for HER2-positive tumors that are ≥2 cm or node-positive, and TNBC ≥2 cm or node-positive. It aims to downsize the tumor and assess response before surgery.
keyPoints: HER2-positive tumors ≥2 cm or node-positive
TNBC ≥2 cm or node-positive
Neoadjuvant therapy with chemotherapy and anti-HER2 agents for HER2-positive
Neoadjuvant therapy with chemotherapy and possibly pembrolizumab for TNBC
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.title: Role of pCR in Neoadjuvant Therapy Outcomes
items.content: Patients achieving a complete pathologic response (pCR) to neoadjuvant therapy have better outcomes, though no survival difference is shown in randomized trials between adjuvant and neoadjuvant approaches.
keyPoints: Complete pathologic response (pCR) correlates with better outcomes
No survival difference in randomized trials comparing adjuvant vs. neoadjuvant therapy
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.title: Use of Endocrine Therapy in Neoadjuvant Settings
items.content: Neoadjuvant endocrine therapy is used for postmenopausal women with ER- or PR-positive breast cancer to potentially reduce surgery extent or avoid it, especially beneficial for older patients with comorbidities.
keyPoints: Postmenopausal women with ER-/PR-positive tumors
Potential reduction in surgery extent or avoidance
Beneficial for older women with multiple comorbidities
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.title: Endocrine Therapy for ER- and/or PR-Positive Breast Cancer
items.content: Endocrine therapy is the cornerstone of adjuvant treatment for women with ER- and/or PR-positive breast cancer. The choice between tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors, or a combination depends on menopausal status and risk level.
keyPoints: Tamoxifen is standard for premenopausal women with low-risk tumors (5–10 years).
Premenopausal women with higher risk may need LHRH agonist plus tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitor for 5 years.
Aromatase inhibitors offer a modest but significant benefit over tamoxifen in postmenopausal women (5-year treatment).
ER-positive patients are at risk for distant recurrence up to 20+ years, with some trials suggesting up to 10 years of therapy for high-risk postmenopausal women.
Side effects: Tamoxifen can cause thromboembolic events or uterine cancer; aromatase inhibitors may lead to arthralgias and bone loss.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.title: Role of Chemotherapy in ER-Positive Breast Cancer
items.content: The decision to add chemotherapy to endocrine therapy depends on menopausal and nodal status, with genomic testing helping identify patients who may not benefit from additional chemotherapy.
keyPoints: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is recommended for large tumors or more than three positive nodes, especially in premenopausal women.
Adjuvant chemotherapy is considered for those with four or more positive lymph nodes or high genomic signature scores.
Long-term side effects include premature menopause, cardiomyopathy, neuropathy, secondary leukemia, and cognitive dysfunction.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.title: Approach to HER2-Positive Breast Cancer
items.content: Neoadjuvant therapy with trastuzumab and pertuzumab is recommended for tumors ≥3 cm or positive lymph nodes, with considerations based on pCR status.
keyPoints: Neoadjuvant therapy includes multiagent chemotherapy plus trastuzumab (and possibly pertuzumab) for 4–5 months.
Patients achieving pCR continue trastuzumab/pertuzumab for a year; those with residual disease switch to TDM-1.
Cardiac evaluation is crucial due to the risk of reduced ejection fraction from trastuzumab.
Pertuzumab may cause diarrhea, managed with loperamide; its benefit over trastuzumab alone is modest.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)
items.title: Treatment Strategy for HER2-Positive Breast Cancer
items.content: For patients with HER2-positive tumors that are also ER and/or PR positive, endocrine therapy should be administered after chemotherapy. It can be given concurrently with anti-HER2 therapy. CDK4/6 inhibitors or PARP inhibitors have not been tested as adjuvant therapies for early-stage HER2-positive breast cancer.
keyPoints: Endocrine therapy is initiated post-chemotherapy and can coincide with anti-HER2 therapy.
CDK4/6 inhibitors and PARP inhibitors are not tested in this context.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)
items.title: Management of Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (TNBC)
items.content: Patients with early-stage TNBC and tumors >2 cm or >1 cm with positive axillary nodes should consider neoadjuvant multiagent chemotherapy, potentially with pembrolizumab. Post-surgery, pembrolizumab continues for a year if chemoimmunotherapy was used. Non-pCR patients may receive capecitabine.
keyPoints: Neoadjuvant therapy is recommended for tumors >2 cm or >1 cm with positive nodes.
Pembrolizumab is continued postoperatively for a year if used initially.
Non-pCR patients can consider further chemotherapy with capecitabine.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.title: Use of Bisphosphonates in Early Breast Cancer Management
items.content: Bisphosphonates such as zoledronate, clodronate, or ibandronate can be used to strengthen bone health compromised by breast cancer therapies like aromatase inhibitors and LHRH agonists. They may also reduce breast cancer recurrence, particularly in the bone.
keyPoints: Bisphosphonates recommended for patients receiving estrogen-deprivation agents.
Zoledronate administered every 6 months for 3 years as a common regimen.
Meta-analysis suggests decreased breast cancer recurrence with bisphosphonate use, especially in bone.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.title: Breast Cancer Diagnosis and Treatment During Pregnancy
items.content: Diagnosing and treating breast cancer during pregnancy requires careful management to balance maternal health and fetal safety. Surgery is safe in the second and third trimesters, while radiation should be delayed until post-delivery. Certain chemotherapy agents can be used safely during later stages of pregnancy.
keyPoints: Ultrasound or MRI preferred for imaging during pregnancy.
Breast surgery feasible in second/third trimesters; avoid therapeutic radiation until after delivery.
Doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide safe in second/third trimesters; methotrexate and 5-fluorouracil should be avoided.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.title: Fertility Preservation in Premenopausal Women Diagnosed with Breast Cancer
items.content: Premenopausal women diagnosed with breast cancer need to discuss fertility preservation, as adjuvant chemotherapy can lead to premature menopause. Concurrent use of LHRH agonists during chemotherapy may help preserve ovarian function without affecting breast cancer outcomes.
keyPoints: Premature menopause risk <50% in women <40 years old; higher in those over 40.
Fertility preservation techniques should be prioritized if future childbearing is desired.
LHRH agonists may help preserve ovarian function during chemotherapy without adverse breast cancer outcomes.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.title: Diagnosis and Evaluation of Inflammatory Breast Cancer
items.content: Inflammatory breast cancer, a subset of locally advanced breast cancer, presents with rapid onset of erythema, swelling, and tenderness. It is crucial to differentiate it from mastitis or other infections. If inflammation persists despite antibiotics, a biopsy is necessary. Skin biopsy revealing dermal lymphatic invasion confirms the diagnosis.
keyPoints: Inflammatory breast cancer presents with erythema, swelling, and tenderness.
Up to one-third of patients have detectable metastasis at diagnosis.
Skin biopsy showing dermal lymphatic invasion is diagnostic.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED BREAST CANCER

items.title: Treatment Approach for Locally Advanced Breast Cancer
items.content: Locally advanced breast cancer often requires combined-modality therapy. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy, guided by biomarker status, is used to downstage the tumor for resection. Mastectomy with axillary dissection is typically necessary due to high recurrence rates with breast-conserving therapy. Postoperative radiotherapy complements systemic therapies like chemotherapy, anti-HER2, and endocrine therapy.
keyPoints: About 10% of patients in the U.S. present with stage III breast cancer.
Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is used to downstage tumors for resection.
Mastectomy with axillary dissection is often required due to high recurrence rates.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED BREAST CANCER

items.title: Detection and Evaluation of Metastatic Breast Cancer
items.content: Metastatic breast cancer can occur in 20–25% of patients treated for early-stage disease. It is often detected years to decades after the initial diagnosis, particularly with ER-positive disease. De novo metastatic presentations are rare (5%). Evaluation involves imaging like CT and radionuclide scans, blood studies, and tumor marker measurements such as CEA, CA27/29, or CA15-3. Biopsy of suspicious lesions is crucial to confirm metastasis and assess changes in markers like ER, PR, HER2, PD-L1, and mutations (e.g., PIK3CA, ESR1).
keyPoints: 20–25% develop metastatic disease post early-stage treatment.
De novo stage IV presentation is about 5%.
ER-positive: longer detection period; TNBC/ER-negative/HER2-positive: 3-7 years.
Imaging and tumor marker assessment critical for evaluation.
Biopsy confirms diagnosis and assesses molecular markers.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.title: Goals of Care in Metastatic Breast Cancer
items.content: Metastatic breast cancer is generally incurable, with a median survival of less than 3 years. Therapy focuses on palliation and symptom prevention without undue toxicity. Patients with ER-positive disease and favorable characteristics may survive longer using serial therapies. Systemic therapy is the primary intervention; surgery is limited to specific cases like isolated recurrences or solitary metastases. Radiotherapy can be used for symptomatic relief of localized disease.
keyPoints: Median survival <3 years, varies widely.
Goals: palliation and symptom prevention.
Systemic therapy is primary; surgery is limited.
Radiotherapy for symptomatic local disease.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.title: Management of ER-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer
items.content: Endocrine therapy is preferred for ER/PR-positive metastatic breast cancer unless the clinical situation is dire. Chemotherapy may be reserved for life-threatening visceral disease, but a trial showed similar survival with endocrine therapy plus CDK4/6 inhibitor versus chemotherapy alone in rapidly progressing cases. Concurrent endocrine and chemotherapy have no added benefit. Endocrine therapy choice depends on menopausal status and prior treatments, potentially combined with targeted therapies based on molecular profiles.
keyPoints: Endocrine therapy preferred for ER/PR-positive metastatic disease.
Chemotherapy reserved for life-threatening visceral disease.
CDK4/6 inhibitors can be used with endocrine therapy.
No benefit from concurrent endocrine and chemotherapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.title: Initial Therapy for Untreated ER-Positive Metastatic Breast Cancer
items.content: For postmenopausal women with untreated ER-positive metastatic breast cancer, therapy typically starts with an aromatase inhibitor plus a CDK4/6 inhibitor. For premenopausal women, the regimen includes an LHRH agonist in addition to the aforementioned agents.
keyPoints: Median progression-free survival is about 2 years.
Median overall survival exceeds 3 years with this approach.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.title: Management of Endocrine-Unresponsive ER-Positive, HER2-Negative Breast Cancer
items.content: When metastatic HER2-negative breast cancer becomes unresponsive to endocrine therapy, chemotherapy becomes the primary systemic treatment. Combination chemotherapy is reserved for cases requiring rapid tumor reduction (visceral crisis), while single-agent chemotherapy is generally preferred due to its acceptable balance of efficacy and toxicity.
keyPoints: Combination chemotherapy does not improve overall survival over sequential monotherapy.
The likelihood of disease control decreases by about one-half with each successive switch in endocrine therapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.question: Which factor is associated with an increased risk of breast cancer due to prolonged estrogen exposure?
options: Early menarche, late menopause, and late first pregnancy
items.explanation: Prolonged estrogen exposure from early menarche, late menopause, and late first pregnancy increases breast cancer risk.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Breast Cancer | NONGENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.question: Which population is most likely to attain the breast cancer risk of a higher incidence region within one or two generations?
options: Populations who migrate from high-incidence regions to low-incidence regions
Non-Hispanic whites in the United States
Immigrant populations moving from low-incidence to high-incidence regions
items.explanation: Immigrant populations who migrate from low-incidence to high-incidence regions attain the higher risk within one or two generations.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Breast Cancer | NONGENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.question: Which benign breast pathology is associated with a 7- to 12-fold increase in breast cancer risk?
items.explanation: A diagnosis of lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) increases breast cancer risk by 7- to 12-fold.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Breast Cancer | NONGENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.question: Which genes are most commonly associated with hereditary breast cancer susceptibility?
items.explanation: The most common mutations associated with hereditary breast cancer are in the BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.question: What is the approximate risk of developing invasive breast cancer by age 80 years for individuals with BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations?
items.explanation: Individuals with BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations have a 50–80% risk of developing invasive breast cancer by age 80 years.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.question: Which population has a higher incidence of BRCA mutations?
items.explanation: Certain populations, especially Ashkenazi Jews, have a higher incidence of BRCA mutations.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.question: What percentage risk reduction of developing breast cancer is associated with prophylactic mastectomy in individuals carrying a germline breast cancer susceptibility gene mutation?
items.explanation: Prophylactic mastectomy reduces the risk of developing breast cancer by about 90% in individuals with a germline mutation.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · PREVENTION | PROPHYLACTIC SURGERY | CHEMOPREVENTION

items.question: Which medication is recommended as an alternative to tamoxifen for postmenopausal women due to its lower association with uterine cancers?
items.explanation: Raloxifene is recommended as an alternative to tamoxifen for postmenopausal women because it is associated with fewer uterine cancers.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · PREVENTION | PROPHYLACTIC SURGERY | CHEMOPREVENTION

items.question: What is the approximate reduction in breast cancer incidence provided by aromatase inhibitors like exemestane or anastrozole in postmenopausal women at moderate-high risk?
items.explanation: Aromatase inhibitors reduce breast cancer incidence by about 50% in postmenopausal women at moderate-high risk.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · PREVENTION | PROPHYLACTIC SURGERY | CHEMOPREVENTION

items.question: Which lifestyle modification has been associated with a nonsignificant reduction in breast cancer incidence but appeared to reduce risk of death from breast cancer according to the dietary substudy of the Women's Health Initiative?
options: Maintenance of a normal body mass index
Minimizing use of hormone replacement therapy
A low-fat diet in postmenopausal women
items.explanation: The dietary substudy showed that a low-fat diet resulted in a nonsignificant reduction in breast cancer incidence but appeared to reduce risk of death from breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION | SCREENING

items.question: Which screening modality has been shown to lead to a significant reduction in the proportion of women diagnosed with stage III or IV breast cancer?
items.explanation: A randomized trial in India showed that CBE every 2 years led to a significant reduction in the proportion of women diagnosed with stage III or IV disease.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION | SCREENING

items.question: According to current guidelines, how often should mammography be performed for women aged 50–70 years?
items.explanation: Most guidelines recommend that women aged 50–70 years have mammography every 1–3 years.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION | SCREENING

items.question: Which of the following is a common finding on mammographic screening that may indicate breast cancer?
options: A mass, distortion, and/or suspicious microcalcifications
Increased breast density without other findings
items.explanation: Abnormal mammographic findings suggestive of breast cancer include a mass, distortion, and/or suspicious microcalcifications.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.question: What is the primary method for confirming a diagnosis of breast cancer?
items.explanation: Final diagnosis of breast cancer rests on pathologic confirmation, generally carried out by image-guided core biopsy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.question: What percentage of women with newly diagnosed breast cancer have unsuspected contralateral disease?
items.explanation: Up to 3% of women with newly diagnosed breast cancer have unsuspected contralateral disease.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.question: Which molecular subtype of invasive breast cancer is characterized by high expression of HER2 protein and is more likely to benefit from HER2-targeted therapies?
items.explanation: The HER2 subtype expresses high levels of the HER2 protein and benefits from HER2-targeted therapies.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.question: Which molecular subtype of invasive breast cancer generally lacks expression of ER, PR, and HER2 proteins?
items.explanation: Triple-negative breast cancers (TNBC) lack expression of ER, PR, and HER2 proteins.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.question: Which genomic transcriptional profiling assay is recommended by the American Society of Clinical Oncology for evaluating early-stage ER-positive breast cancers?
items.explanation: The American Society of Clinical Oncology recommends several assays, including Oncotype Dx, MammaPrint, and ProSigna.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.question: What is the typical management approach for triple-negative breast cancers (TNBC)?
items.explanation: Triple-negative breast cancers are generally managed with chemotherapy and immunotherapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER
items.question: What is the typical management strategy for Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS) to prevent invasive cancer?
options: Excision of the lesion with radiotherapy and endocrine therapy if ER-positive.
Routine use of screening mammography alone.
Evaluation of axillary lymph nodes followed by chemotherapy.
items.explanation: The management focuses on excision to negative margins, with radiotherapy and endocrine therapy if ER-positive.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical treatment for Lobular Carcinoma In Situ (LCIS)?
options: Chemoprevention with tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors.
Bilateral prophylactic mastectomy in high-risk patients.
items.explanation: Axillary lymph node evaluation is not typically part of LCIS management.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER
items.question: In the context of DCIS, what is the purpose of molecular assays?
options: To predict benefit from radiotherapy after excision.
To determine the need for immediate chemotherapy.
To evaluate axillary lymph node involvement.
To assess the risk of contralateral breast cancer.
items.explanation: Molecular assays are used to predict lack of benefit from radiotherapy after excision.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a contraindication to breast-conservation therapy in early-stage breast cancer?
options: Germline mutation in BRCA gene family
items.explanation: A germline mutation in the BRCA gene family is not an absolute contraindication but can influence decision-making.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.question: What is a current strategy for managing the ipsilateral axilla in patients with small tumors and clinically negative nodes?
items.explanation: Sentinel node localization is used to identify relevant nodes for removal and pathologic assessment.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.question: Which of the following radiotherapy options has failed to demonstrate benefit over whole breast radiotherapy (WBR) in clinical trials?
options: Standard scheme of daily WBR for 4–6 weeks
items.explanation: Clinical trials have failed to demonstrate a benefit of partial breast radiation over whole breast radiotherapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.question: For which patients is the safety of omitting postoperative radiotherapy demonstrated?
options: Women younger than 65 with high-risk tumors
Older women with low-risk ER-positive tumors taking endocrine therapy
Patients with large tumors or positive axillary lymph nodes
Individuals with substantial pathologic involvement of sentinel nodes
items.explanation: Omitting radiotherapy is safe for older women with low-risk ER-positive tumors who will take endocrine therapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.question: What is a primary goal of neoadjuvant therapy in the treatment of breast cancer?
options: To increase tumor size before surgery.
To downsize the tumor to make it more amenable to surgery.
To avoid any form of systemic therapy.
items.explanation: Neoadjuvant therapy aims to reduce tumor size, making surgical removal easier.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.question: Which patients are considered for neoadjuvant therapy with chemotherapy and anti-HER2 agents?
options: Patients with HER2-negative tumors that are ≥ 1 cm.
Patients with HER2-positive tumors that are ≥ 2 cm or node-positive disease.
All patients regardless of tumor size or type.
Only postmenopausal women with ER-positive tumors.
items.explanation: Neoadjuvant therapy with anti-HER2 agents is considered for HER2-positive tumors ≥ 2 cm or node-positive disease.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.question: What type of systemic therapy can be used in premenopausal women to reduce circulating estradiol levels?
items.explanation: GnRH agonists or surgical oophorectomy can reduce estradiol levels in premenopausal women.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.question: Which agent is FDA-approved for early-stage TNBC as an immunotherapy?
items.explanation: Pembrolizumab is FDA-approved for early-stage TNBC as an immunotherapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.question: What is the standard adjuvant endocrine therapy for premenopausal women with low-risk ER- and/or PR-positive breast cancer?
options: LHRH agonist plus tamoxifen for 3 years
items.explanation: Tamoxifen for 5–10 years is the standard adjuvant endocrine therapy for premenopausal women with low-risk ER- and/or PR-positive breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is a side effect associated with tamoxifen in postmenopausal women?
items.explanation: Tamoxifen is associated with a small chance of thromboembolic events or uterine cancer in postmenopausal women.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.question: What is the recommended duration of abemaciclib use in conjunction with endocrine therapy for ER- and/or PR-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer after adjuvant chemotherapy?
items.explanation: Current information supports the use of abemaciclib for 2 years in conjunction with endocrine therapy after adjuvant chemotherapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.question: Which genomic multigene expression testing assay is NOT mentioned as being used to identify patients who would not benefit from adjuvant chemotherapy?
items.explanation: The passage mentions Oncotype, MammaPrint, ProSigna, EndoPredict, and Breast Cancer Index as assays for genomic multigene expression testing.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)
items.question: Which regimen is recommended for women with pathologically node-negative tumors <3 cm?
options: Single-agent weekly paclitaxel for 12 weeks and a year of trastuzumab
CDK4/6 inhibitors followed by PARP inhibitors
items.explanation: Single-agent weekly paclitaxel for 12 weeks and a year of trastuzumab is recommended for women with pathologically node-negative tumors <3 cm.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)
items.question: What therapy should be initiated after chemotherapy in patients with HER2-positive tumors that are ER and/or PR positive?
items.explanation: Endocrine therapy is initiated after chemotherapy in patients with HER2-positive tumors that are ER and/or PR positive.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)
items.question: What is the follow-up frequency for asymptomatic survivors of early-stage breast cancer in the first 3 years?
items.explanation: Asymptomatic survivors are followed every 3–6 months for the first 3 years.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.question: Which bisphosphonate is recommended every 6 months for 3 years to help maintain bone health and potentially decrease breast cancer recurrence?
items.explanation: Zoledronate is recommended every 6 months for 3 years as a bone-strengthening agent and may decrease breast cancer recurrence.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.question: During which trimesters can certain chemotherapy agents such as doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide be safely administered to pregnant women?
items.explanation: Certain chemotherapy agents like doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide can be administered during the second and third trimesters.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.question: Which therapy should be avoided until after delivery when managing breast cancer during pregnancy?
options: Chemotherapy agents like doxorubicin and cyclophosphamide
items.explanation: Endocrine and targeted therapies should not be used until after delivery during pregnancy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.question: What percentage of men with breast cancer have a germline BRCA mutation?
items.explanation: Up to 14% of men with breast cancer have a germline BRCA mutation.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.question: Which clinical finding is often associated with inflammatory breast cancer?
options: Dermal lymphatic invasion with tumor cells
Absence of axillary lymph node involvement
items.explanation: Skin biopsy showing dermal lymphatic invasion with tumor cells is often associated with inflammatory breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED BREAST CANCER

items.question: What percentage of patients with locally advanced breast cancer may have detectable metastasis at the time of diagnosis?
items.explanation: Up to one-third (approximately 33%) of patients with locally advanced breast cancer may have detectable metastasis at the time of diagnosis.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED BREAST CANCER

items.question: Why is breast-conserving therapy generally not recommended for T4 lesions in locally advanced breast cancer?
options: It has a high incidence of locoregional recurrence.
It is always associated with metastatic disease.
It does not require neoadjuvant systemic therapy.
items.explanation: Breast-conserving therapy has been associated with an unacceptably high incidence of locoregional recurrence in T4 lesions, making mastectomy often required.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED BREAST CANCER

items.question: Approximately what percentage of patients treated for early breast cancer develop metastatic disease?
items.explanation: About 20–25% of patients who are treated for early breast cancer subsequently develop metastatic disease.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.question: When is metastatic disease most likely to be diagnosed after treatment for TNBC or ER-negative/HER2-positive breast cancer?
items.explanation: Metastatic disease is most likely to be diagnosed within 3–7 years after treatment for TNBC or ER-negative/HER2-positive breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended intervention in metastatic breast cancer?
options: Surgery for primary breast cancer with systemic therapy
items.explanation: Randomized trials suggest that patients with de novo metastatic breast cancer do not have improved outcomes with surgical treatment of the primary breast cancer in addition to systemic therapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.question: What is the preferred approach for managing ER-positive metastatic breast cancer when the clinical presentation is not dire?
items.explanation: Whenever possible, serial use of endocrine therapy is the preferred approach for patients whose recurrent tumor is ER and/or PR positive.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.question: What is the initial therapy for postmenopausal women with untreated ER-positive metastatic breast cancer?
items.explanation: The initial therapy for postmenopausal women with untreated ER-positive metastatic breast cancer is an aromatase inhibitor plus CDK4/6 inhibitor.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.question: Which agent can be considered for postmenopausal patients previously treated with endocrine therapy and found to have a PIK3CA mutation?
items.explanation: For postmenopausal patients with a PIK3CA mutation, alpelisib (a selective inhibitor of PI3Kα) can be considered with fulvestrant.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended approach for managing metastatic HER2-negative breast cancer that is not responsive to endocrine therapy?
options: Single-agent chemotherapy with possible disease palliation
items.explanation: For metastatic HER2-negative breast cancer unresponsive to endocrine therapy, single-agent chemotherapy is associated with acceptable disease palliation without excess toxicity.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.statement: Premenopausal obesity is a known risk factor for breast cancer.
items.explanation: Postmenopausal, not premenopausal, obesity is associated with increased breast cancer risk.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Breast Cancer | NONGENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.statement: Alcohol consumption has been conclusively shown to increase the risk of breast cancer.
items.explanation: Alcohol consumption is a documented risk factor for breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Breast Cancer | NONGENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.statement: Germline testing for breast cancer susceptibility is recommended for the general population.
items.explanation: Testing for germline mutations is not recommended for the general population.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.statement: BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations are transmitted in an autosomal recessive fashion.
items.explanation: Inherited mutations of BRCA1 and BRCA2 are transmitted in an autosomal dominant fashion.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENETIC RISK FACTORS

items.statement: Prophylactic oophorectomy is primarily used for breast cancer prevention in premenopausal women.
items.explanation: The primary role of prophylactic oophorectomy is ovarian cancer prevention, although it can reduce breast cancer incidence by about 50% in premenopausal women.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · PREVENTION | PROPHYLACTIC SURGERY | CHEMOPREVENTION

items.statement: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends offering risk-reducing medications to women at increased risk for breast cancer and low risk for medication side effects.
items.explanation: The USPSTF does recommend offering tamoxifen, raloxifene, or aromatase inhibitors to women at increased risk for breast cancer who are also at low risk for medication side effects.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · PREVENTION | PROPHYLACTIC SURGERY | CHEMOPREVENTION

items.statement: The role of newer GLP-1 agents in reducing breast cancer risk has been well studied.
items.explanation: The passage states that the role of newer GLP-1 agents has not been studied.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION | SCREENING

items.statement: Screening mammography reduced breast cancer mortality by about 20–25% in women ≥50 years.
items.explanation: Nine randomized trials have demonstrated that screening mammography reduced breast cancer mortality by about 20–25% in women ≥50 years.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LIFESTYLE MODIFICATION | SCREENING

items.statement: MRI is universally recommended for all patients after a biopsy-proven diagnosis of breast cancer.
items.explanation: The use of MRI after a biopsy-proven diagnosis is controversial due to its sensitivity and potential for leading to additional surgery without clear outcome improvements.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.statement: Less than 10% of patients present with de novo metastatic breast cancer.
items.explanation: The passage states that less than 10% of patients present with de novo metastatic breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · DIAGNOSIS AND STAGING

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.statement: Lobular histologies of invasive breast cancer are usually HER2 positive.
items.explanation: Lobular cancers are usually ER positive and HER2 negative.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.statement: Mucinous and pure medullary breast cancers have very favorable prognoses with local therapy only.
items.explanation: These subtypes are associated with very favorable prognoses when treated with local therapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER
items.statement: Untreated DCIS has a risk of developing invasive cancer in the same breast.
items.explanation: Untreated DCIS is associated with a 30% risk of subsequent invasive cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER
items.statement: Evaluation of axillary lymph nodes is routinely performed in the management of DCIS.
items.explanation: Axillary lymph node evaluation is not generally undertaken for DCIS.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER

items.statement: Breast-conservation therapy and modified radical mastectomy have shown equivalent survival in randomized trials for early-stage breast cancer.
items.explanation: Four randomized trials have demonstrated equivalent survival with these two treatment options.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.statement: Axillary dissection is routinely performed for all patients with early-stage breast cancer.
items.explanation: Axillary dissection is restricted to individuals with palpable lymph nodes or substantial pathologic involvement of sentinel nodes.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.statement: Randomized trials have shown a difference in survival between adjuvant and neoadjuvant therapy.
items.explanation: No randomized trials of adjuvant versus neoadjuvant therapy have shown a difference in survival.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.statement: Neoadjuvant endocrine therapy can be used for postmenopausal women with ER- or PR-positive breast cancer.
items.explanation: Neoadjuvant endocrine therapy is indeed used for postmenopausal women with ER- or PR-positive breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.statement: The likelihood of a pathologic complete response (pCR) is high in ER-positive disease treated with neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
items.explanation: In ER-positive disease, the likelihood of a pCR is low.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.statement: Trastuzumab therapy should be discontinued if there is evidence of reduced cardiac ejection fraction.
items.explanation: Trastuzumab should not be given to those with low ejection fraction, and serial echocardiograms are needed during therapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)
items.statement: Serial echocardiograms beyond 12 months of trastuzumab therapy are needed.
items.explanation: Serial echocardiograms beyond 12 months of trastuzumab therapy are not needed.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)
items.statement: Routine imaging of other types or blood studies enhances wellbeing or outcome from breast cancer.
items.explanation: Routine imaging of other types or blood studies have not been shown to enhance wellbeing or outcome.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.statement: Premature menopause is common in women over 40 who undergo adjuvant chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Premature menopause is more common in those over 40 undergoing adjuvant chemotherapy.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.statement: There is evidence that pregnancy after early stage breast cancer diagnosis increases the rate of recurrence.
items.explanation: There is no evidence of increased rate of recurrence for pregnancies after early stage breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.statement: Inflammatory breast cancer can be confused with mastitis.
items.explanation: Inflammatory tumors in breast cancer can present similarly to mastitis, leading to potential confusion.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED BREAST CANCER

items.statement: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is not used for locally advanced breast cancer.
items.explanation: Neoadjuvant systemic therapy is used to downstage tumors in locally advanced breast cancer, allowing for resection.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · LOCALLY ADVANCED BREAST CANCER

items.statement: Metastatic breast cancer can be detected years to decades after primary diagnosis, especially in the setting of ER-positive disease.
items.explanation: Metastatic disease can indeed be detected years to decades after primary diagnosis, particularly with ER-positive disease.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.statement: Concurrent endocrine therapy and chemotherapy is recommended for managing metastatic breast cancer.
items.explanation: There is no value for concurrent endocrine therapy and chemotherapy in the management of metastatic breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.statement: The use of combination chemotherapy in metastatic breast cancer improves overall survival over sequential monotherapy.
items.explanation: Combination chemotherapy does not improve overall survival over sequential monotherapy in metastatic breast cancer.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.statement: Patients with ESR1 mutation may be candidates for the oral SERD agent elacestrant if they have also received a CDK4/6 inhibitor.
items.explanation: Patients with an ESR1 mutation who have received a CDK4/6 inhibitor can be considered for elacestrant.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 2)

items.assertion: Prophylactic mastectomy is recommended for women with a low or modest risk of breast cancer.
items.reason: Prophylactic mastectomy significantly reduces the risk of developing breast cancer.
items.explanation: Prophylactic mastectomy is not recommended for women with only low or modest risk as it does not improve outcomes in these cases.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · PREVENTION | PROPHYLACTIC SURGERY | CHEMOPREVENTION

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.assertion: Luminal B tumors have a poorer outcome than luminal A tumors.
items.reason: Luminal B tumors are characterized by reduced PR expression or increased proliferation, usually measured by Ki67 expression.
items.explanation: The characteristics of Luminal B tumors explain their association with a poorer outcome.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS
items.assertion: Metastatic tumor specimens are often analyzed for molecular alterations to identify targeted therapies.
items.reason: Detailed molecular analysis is usually restricted to metastatic tumor specimens.
items.explanation: Molecular profiling of metastatic tumors helps in identifying specific targeted therapy options.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · CURRENT UNDERSTANDING OF BREAST CANCER BIOLOGY AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER
items.assertion: LCIS management includes consideration of chemoprevention with tamoxifen or aromatase inhibitors.
items.reason: LCIS is a marker for increased risk of developing invasive breast cancer.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · MANAGEMENT OF IN SITU BREAST CANCER

items.assertion: Systemic therapy in early-stage breast cancer may include neoadjuvant and adjuvant treatments.
items.reason: Breast cancer is often a systemic disease at the time of diagnosis, necessitating comprehensive treatment approaches.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why systemic therapy may be used preoperatively or postoperatively.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.assertion: Immediate mastectomy is required for all patients with early-stage breast cancer.
items.reason: Mastectomy is the only option when there are contraindications to breast conservation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; immediate mastectomy is not required for all, and while mastectomy may be chosen due to contraindications, it's not the sole option.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · TREATMENT OF EARLY-STAGE BREAST CANCER

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.assertion: Patients who achieve a complete pathologic response to neoadjuvant therapy have better outcomes.
items.reason: Data suggest that achieving pCR correlates with improved patient outcomes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY
items.assertion: Aromatase inhibitors can be used as monotherapy in premenopausal women.
items.reason: Aromatase inhibitors are restricted to postmenopausal women.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because aromatase inhibitors cannot be used as monotherapy in premenopausal women.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · SELECTION OF NEOADJUVANT VERSUS ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.assertion: Healthy individuals with HER2-positive breast cancer and tumor size ≥3 cm are candidates for neoadjuvant therapy.
items.reason: Neoadjuvant therapy includes multiagent chemotherapy and trastuzumab with or without pertuzumab for 4–5 months.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these individuals are candidates.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)
items.assertion: Aromatase inhibitors should be avoided in premenopausal women due to severe side effects.
items.reason: Aromatase inhibitors are associated with postmenopausal symptoms, arthralgias, and bone loss.
items.explanation: The assertion is true as aromatase inhibitors are generally avoided in premenopausal women, but the reason given relates to side effects seen in postmenopausal women.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · GENERAL GUIDELINES FOR (NEO)ADJUVANT SYSTEMIC THERAPY FOR EARLY BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.assertion: Concurrent administration of an LHRH agonist during adjuvant chemotherapy may help preserve ovarian function.
items.reason: A meta-analysis suggests that this approach does not adversely affect breast cancer outcomes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.subtopic: Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer
items.assertion: Oral contraceptives should be used by premenopausal breast cancer survivors for contraception.
items.reason: They do not affect breast cancer outcomes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; oral contraceptives are recommended to be avoided, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · Special Considerations in Management of Individuals with Early Breast Cancer

items.assertion: Patients with de novo metastatic breast cancer have improved outcomes with surgical treatment for the primary breast cancer.
items.reason: Randomized trials suggest that patients who present with de novo metastatic breast cancer do not have improved outcomes with surgical treatment of the primary breast cancer in addition to systemic therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason correctly explains why it's false.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.assertion: Overall survival was better in patients with rapidly growing visceral disease assigned to chemotherapy than those given endocrine therapy and a CDK4/6 inhibitor.
items.reason: A prospective trial suggested that overall survival was the same in patients with rapidly growing visceral disease randomly assigned to either chemotherapy or endocrine therapy and a CDK4/6 inhibitor.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason correctly states that there was no difference in overall survival between the two treatment groups.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 1)

items.assertion: The likelihood of disease control and duration of benefit decrease with each successive switch in endocrine therapy.
items.reason: This is due to the tumor becoming unresponsive to endocrine therapy over time.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why disease control decreases with successive switches.
items.reference: Breast Cancer · METASTATIC BREAST CANCER (part 2)
